corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: In the ultra-low resource setup with just 10 minutes of labeled data, the HuBERT LARGE model can achieve a WER of 4.7% on the test-clean set and 7.6% on the test-other set, which is 0. and .6% WER lower, respectively than the state-of-the-art wav2vec 2.0 LARGE model.
In addition, HuBERT also outperforms DiscreteBERT by a large margin in all setups, while both are trained with a virtually identical objective -masked prediction of discovered units.
HuBERT outperforms the state-of-the-art supervised and selftraining methods and is on par with the two best pre-training results in the literature; both are based on wav2vec 2.0 contrastive learning.
HuBERT outperforms the state-of-the-art supervised and selftraining methods and is on par with the two best pre-training results in the literature; both are based on wav2vec 2.0 contrastive learning.
As a baseline, MFCC achieves (cluster purity, phone purity, PNMI) = (0.099, 0.335, 0.255) for K = 100 and (0.031, 0.356, 0.287) for K = 500.

Results shown in Table indicate that when learning from bad cluster assignments, computing loss only from the masked regions achieves the best performance, while the inclusion of unmasked loss results in significantly higher WERs.

It is shown that (1) the portion of frames selected as mask start is optimal at p =8%; (2) increasing the batch size can significantly improve the performance; (3) training for longer consistently helps for both k-means models with C={50, 100}, and the best model achieves a WER of 11.68%.

Besides using continuous speech input rather than discrete units, We hypothesize that HuBERT achieves significantly better performance because its fewer k-means clusters of 100 or 500 help capture broad phonetic concepts without delving into inter/intra-speaker variation.
For future work, we plan to improve the HuBERT training procedure to consist of a single phase.

To deal with these three problems, we propose the Hidden-Unit BERT (HuBERT) approach for self-supervised speech representation learning, which utilizes an offline clustering step to provide aligned target labels for a BERT-like prediction loss.
In this paper, we introduce Hidden unit BERT (HuBERT) that benefits from an offline clustering step to generate noisy labels for a BERT-like per-training.

When the HuBERT model is pre-trained on either the standard Librispeech 960h or the Libri-Light 60k hours , it either matches or improves upon the state-of-theart wav2vec 2.0 performance on all fine-tuning subsets of 10mins, 1h, 10h, 100h, and 960h.
We present systematic results on three model sizes pre-trained with HuBERT: BASE (90M parameters), LARGE (300M), and X-LARGE (1B).
The X-LARGE model shows up to 19% and 13% relative WER improvement from LARGE models on dev-other and test-other evaluation subsets when pre-trained on the Libri-Light 60k hours.
Second, we also present many techniques to improve teacher quality instead of using a single fixed teacher as done in DiscreteBERT.

However, the latter employs a contrastive loss that requires careful design of where to sample negative frames from, an auxiliary diversity loss to encourage the discrete unit usage, and demands a proper Gumbel-softmax temperature annealing schedule.

Concretely, our proposed method adopts a more direct predictive loss by separating the acoustic unit discovery step from the masked prediction representation learning phase and achieves the stateof-the-art results that match or outperform wav2vec 2.0 on different fine-tuning scales.

Concretely, our proposed method adopts a more direct predictive loss by separating the acoustic unit discovery step from the masked prediction representation learning phase and achieves the stateof-the-art results that match or outperform wav2vec 2.0 on different fine-tuning scales.

We start with probing the stability of the k-means clustering algorithm concerning different numbers of clusters and We next study how each layer of the HuBERT model from each iteration performs when used for clustering to generate training targets.

Interestingly, the quality of the last few layers degrades dramatically for BASE-it1, potentially because it is trained on target assignments of worse quality, and therefore the last few layers learn to mimic their bad label behavior.
We present a series of ablation studies in the following sections to learn how pre-training objective, cluster quality, and hyperparameters affect the performance.
The second one has k-means models trained on spliced MFCC features with a window of three; hence, each input feature is represented as a 117-dimensional vector.

On both the Librispeech 960 hours and the 60,000 hours Libri-light pretraining setups, HuBERT matches or outperforms the stateof-the-art systems over all fine-tuning subsets of 10mins, 1h, teacher C dev-other WER (%) steps=100k 250k 400k 800k K-means 50 18.68 13.65 12.40 11.82 100 17.86 12.97 12.32 11.68 [51] 13.5k 26.6 TABLE VII: Varying the number of HuBERT pre-training steps. p is set to 6.5%.
